questions.question: How many active slaves can a single Bluetooth piconet support simultaneously?
options: Up to 3 active slaves
Up to 7 active slaves
Up to 15 active slaves
Up to 255 active slaves
questions.explanation: A Bluetooth piconet supports exactly one master and up to seven active slaves. An additional 255 nodes may exist in the parked state, but they cannot transmit data until the master activates them.

questions.question: What characteristic makes SCO (Synchronous Connection Oriented) links unsuitable for file transfers?
options: SCO links use frequency hopping that is too slow for large payloads
SCO links never retransmit lost frames — dropped audio samples are simply discarded
SCO links are limited to 240 bits of payload per slot
SCO links require a separate master for each slave pair
questions.explanation: SCO links are designed for isochronous, real-time audio where latency matters more than reliability. A retransmitted audio sample would arrive too late to be useful, so lost frames are permanently discarded. File transfer requires reliable delivery and therefore uses ACL links with stop-and-wait ARQ.

questions.question: In Bluetooth's Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH), what is the regulatory minimum number of usable channels required before AFH can operate?
options: 10 channels
20 channels
40 channels
79 channels
questions.explanation: AFH requires at least 20 usable (good) channels in the hop sequence. This minimum is mandated by the Bluetooth specification to ensure adequate frequency diversity. If Wi-Fi occupies enough of the 2.4 GHz band to reduce good channels below 20, AFH cannot legally operate.

questions.question: Why does a single-slot Bluetooth SCO frame carrying an 80-bit PCM payload achieve only about 13% efficiency?
options: The 3× repetition coding triples the payload size, reducing usable bits to 13%
The settling time (~250 µs), 72-bit access code, and 54-bit coded header together consume about 60% of the 625-µs slot, leaving only 80/625 ≈ 13% for payload
Bluetooth encrypts the payload with AES-128, expanding it from 80 to 600 bits
The master must wait for the slave acknowledgment within the same slot, halving usable time
questions.explanation: A 625 µs slot at 1 Mbps holds 625 bits. Of those, roughly 250 bits are consumed by radio settling time, 72 bits by the access code, and 54 bits by the 3× repetition-coded header. The remaining 80 bits of PCM payload represent 80/625 ≈ 12.8%, which rounds to the 13% figure cited in the lesson.

questions.question: A Bluetooth master transmits a 54-bit coded header to a slave. The receiver detects that one of the three header copies is entirely corrupted. Which technique allows the correct 18-bit header to be recovered?
options: CRC retransmission: the slave sends a NAK and the master retransmits the header
Majority-vote decoding: for each of the 18 bit positions, the value held by at least 2 of the 3 copies wins
Reed-Solomon forward error correction applied to the concatenated 54-bit word
Hamming distance lookup against a precomputed table of valid 54-bit codewords
questions.explanation: Bluetooth repeats the 18-bit header three times to form a 54-bit coded word. The receiver performs bit-by-bit majority voting across the three copies: if at least two copies agree on a bit value, that value is taken as correct. A single corrupted copy cannot change the majority on any bit, so the original header is fully recovered with no retransmission needed.

questions.question: A device wants to join two piconets simultaneously as a bridge node (scatternet). What must it do differently compared to a node in a single piconet?
options: It must operate at double the transmit power to reach both masters
It must time-share between the two piconets, using each piconet's own master clock and hop sequence when active in that piconet
It must act as the master in at least one of the two piconets to bridge them
It must reduce its slot duration from 625 µs to 312.5 µs to fit into both piconets
questions.explanation: A scatternet bridge node participates in two independent piconets, each governed by its own master's clock and pseudorandom hop sequence. The bridge must time-share — it can only be active in one piconet per slot — and it must re-synchronize to the correct master clock and hop sequence each time it switches. This overhead and synchronization complexity is why scatternets were rarely deployed in practice.
